# MLX-Gen Full Documentation Context

## Project Summary

MLX-Gen is an MLX-native generative image and video runtime forked from mflux and published as the `mlx-gen` Python package. It is currently built on the mflux codebase while adding independent release cadence, Qwen Image/Edit support, ERNIE Image Turbo support, Bonsai low-bit FLUX.2 support, Wan2.2 TI2V-5B and A14B video support, FLUX.2 fixes, quantized model packaging, local model loading behavior, generated Hugging Face model cards, and AbstractVision integration needs.

Some internals currently remain under `mflux.*`; new application code should use the `mlxgen` command and import path.

## Document Index

- `README.md`: installation, quick start, model families, project relationship, and acknowledgements.
- `docs/getting-started.md`: install, inspect `mlxgen --help`, prepare model files, generate images, edit images, and run Wan video examples with duration/FPS/resolution guidance.
- `docs/architecture.md`: package shape, command boundaries, model-file lifecycle, runtime failure contract, and Python integration boundary.
- `docs/api.md`: public `mlxgen` command surface, generation router, model-management commands, and Python integration boundary.
- `docs/model-management.md`: cache-only runtime policy, `download` versus `prepare`, Depth Pro, and `DownloadRequiredError`.
- `docs/quantization.md`: MLX-Gen low-bit compatibility by model family, validation panels, Bonsai ternary 2-bit support, current Qwen/ERNIE mixed q4/q8 policies, Wan mixed q8/BF16 package measurements, and binary 1-bit runtime limits.
- `docs/huggingface-publishing.md`: generated model cards, source license/access wording, default `AbstractFramework/<repo-name>` usage, upload flow, and optional collection membership.
- `docs/python-integration.md`: in-process use, AbstractVision integration, shared progress callbacks, and threading guidance.
- `docs/adr/README.md`: accepted architecture decisions, including model-backed smoke validation requirements for new model routes.
- `docs/faq.md`: common questions about `prepare`, downloads, package naming, compatibility, and Wan image-to-video prompting.
- `docs/troubleshooting.md`: missing files, local path classification, output replacement, `generate --path`, ERNIE Prompt Enhancer files, ERNIE small-resolution and unsupported edit-input behavior, Wan video quality-setting and prompting limits, LoRA, and `hf_transfer`.
- `docs/release.md`: GitHub Release and PyPI trusted publishing workflow.
- `ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS.md`: upstream mflux credit and post-fork maintainership.
- `CONTRIBUTING.md`: development setup, checks, documentation expectations, and pull-request scope.
- `SECURITY.md`: vulnerability reporting and model/token safety guidance.
- `CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md`: participation expectations.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: user-visible release history.

---

## Relationship To mflux

MLX-Gen started as a fork of `filipstrand/mflux`. Most credit for the current codebase goes to Filip Strand and the original mflux contributors. Changes after the fork are maintained in this project. The project keeps mflux vocabulary where useful for compatibility and possible upstream merge-back, while it may diverge as its own package.

See `ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS.md` for upstream mflux credit, post-fork maintainership, and model/community acknowledgements.

---

## Architecture

MLX-Gen keeps the MLX-native model runtime inherited from mflux while exposing a cleaner `mlxgen` command surface for new users and applications. The supported video paths include Wan2.2 TI2V-5B text-to-video, experimental TI2V-5B first-frame image-to-video, Wan2.2 A14B text-to-video with two-transformer boundary routing, and Wan2.2 A14B image-to-video.

ADR 0001 requires model-backed runtime smoke proof before new model routes are described as
working or release-ready. Shape-only tests, mocked CLI tests, and successful weight-load tests are
not enough for a support claim.

The package shape is:

- PyPI distribution: `mlx-gen`
- Public CLI root: `mlxgen`
- New application import identity: `mlxgen`
- Current runtime internals: primarily `mflux.*`

The public command boundary separates setup from inference:

- `mlxgen download` explicitly populates the local cache.
- `mlxgen prepare` explicitly creates a local model folder.
- `mlxgen generate` runs inference and does not start downloads by default.

Prepared folders use the MLX/mflux saved-weight layout and may contain MLX quantization tensors. They are intended for MLX-Gen and compatible mflux code, not direct Diffusers or Transformers loading.

The Python integration boundary exposes lightweight progress monitoring through
`mflux.callbacks.ProgressEvent`; events do not include latents or model tensors.

---

## Runtime Download Policy

MLX-Gen generation is cache-only by default. It will not download model weights, tokenizers, LoRAs, or Depth Pro weights during generation or ordinary Python model construction.

This is required for embedded workflow engines such as AbstractVision: a complex job should not unexpectedly start a large network transfer. Missing files raise `DownloadRequiredError`, which is also a `FileNotFoundError` for compatibility.

The exception exposes:

- `repo_id`
- `artifact`
- `download_command`
- `prepare_command` when a local prepared folder is applicable

---

## Public CLI

The public command surface is:

- `mlxgen generate`: generate images, edit images, or generate supported videos from cached or prepared files.
- `mlxgen download`: explicitly download a model snapshot, LoRA repository, or supported direct-download artifact into the local cache.
- `mlxgen prepare`: create a reusable local MLX-Gen model folder, optionally quantized, and write a Hugging Face `README.md` model card.

Use `mlxgen --help` for the top-level command list and `mlxgen <command> --help` for command-specific options.

`mlxgen prepare` is the public MLX-Gen workflow for creating local quantized model folders. There is no separate MLX-Gen `save` workflow in the user-facing documentation.

---

## Explicit Model Management

Download a Hugging Face snapshot into the local cache:

```sh
mlxgen download --model Qwen/Qwen-Image
```

Download a known alias:

```sh
mlxgen download --model z-image-turbo
```

Prepare a reusable local MLX-Gen folder:

```sh
mlxgen prepare \
  --model Qwen/Qwen-Image \
  --path ./models/qwen-image-8bit \
  --quantize 8
```

Download Depth Pro weights from Apple:

```sh
mlxgen download --model depth-pro
```

`mlxgen download` only fills the local cache. `mlxgen prepare` creates a reusable local folder at `--path`, applies the requested quantization when `--quantize` is provided, and writes a generated model card. The model card records the `mlx-gen` version that generated it, defaults usage examples to `AbstractFramework/<repo-name>`, and includes `python -m pip install -U mlx-gen` for copy/pasteable public installation.

`HF_HUB_ENABLE_HF_TRANSFER=1` is optional acceleration for explicit Hugging Face download/prepare commands. It is not required to authorize network access.

Bonsai Image checkpoints are already packed MLX artifacts. Cache Bonsai with `mlxgen download`
and generate directly; do not use `mlxgen prepare` for Bonsai.

---

## Generation

Use `mlxgen generate` as the smart router:

```sh
mlxgen generate \
  --model z-image-turbo \
  --prompt "A puffin standing on a cliff" \
  --width 1280 \
  --height 500 \
  --seed 42 \
  --steps 9 \
  --quantize 8
```

For image editing:

```sh
mlxgen generate \
  --model AbstractFramework/qwen-image-edit-2511-4bit \
  --image input.png \
  --prompt "Turn the room into a pencil sketch" \
  --steps 20 \
  --seed 42 \
  --output edited.png
```

If a local model path or custom repository name cannot be classified, pass `--family qwen`, `--family flux2`, `--family bonsai`, `--family fibo`, `--family z-image`, `--family ernie-image`, or `--family wan`.

Bonsai Image is routed by `mlxgen generate` for text-to-image:

```sh
mlxgen generate \
  --model prism-ml/bonsai-image-ternary-4B-mlx-2bit \
  --prompt "A bonsai tree in a quiet ceramic studio, soft morning light" \
  --width 1024 \
  --height 1024 \
  --steps 4 \
  --guidance 1 \
  --output bonsai.png
```

Bonsai ternary 2-bit is supported as a pre-packed Prism checkpoint with a low-bit transformer,
4-bit Qwen3 text encoder, and BF16 Flux2 VAE. Bonsai rejects `prepare`, `--quantize`, negative
prompts, and image inputs. Binary 1-bit is detected but rejected until the active MLX runtime can
execute the required `bits=1, group_size=128` packed affine matmul; stock MLX 0.31.2 still rejects
`bits=1`.

ERNIE Image Turbo is also routed by `mlxgen generate`:

```sh
mlxgen generate \
  --model baidu/ERNIE-Image-Turbo \
  --prompt "A clean product photo of a ceramic mug" \
  --width 512 \
  --height 512 \
  --steps 8 \
  --guidance 1 \
  --output image.png
```

For local ERNIE folders whose names cannot be classified, pass `--family ernie-image`. The ERNIE port supports text-to-image plus experimental single-image image-to-image with BF16 source weights and prepared q8/q4 folders. ERNIE Prompt Enhancer is available with `--use-prompt-enhancer` when a full source snapshot with `pe/` and `pe_tokenizer/` files is present. ERNIE multi-image edit is not supported. Width or height below 384px is not recommended because very small ERNIE outputs can crop or truncate subjects. For ERNIE image-to-image, preserve the source aspect ratio, use `--image-strength 0.25` to `0.35` for visible stylization, `0.45` to `0.6` for stronger source preservation, and Qwen Image Edit when exact source layout preservation matters.

`mlxgen generate` does not accept `--path` to create model folders. Use `mlxgen prepare --model ... --path ... --quantize ...` for model preparation and use `--output` to choose the generated image or video path.

Wan2.2 TI2V-5B routes through the same command surface for text-to-video. Use the upstream TI2V-5B scale for visual quality validation:

```sh
mlxgen generate \
  --model Wan-AI/Wan2.2-TI2V-5B-Diffusers \
  --task text-to-video \
  --prompt "A short cinematic video of a glowing orange glass sphere floating above teal water" \
  --width 1280 \
  --height 704 \
  --frames 121 \
  --steps 50 \
  --guidance 5 \
  --fps 24 \
  --output video.mp4
```

Wan image-to-video uses model-specific Diffusers conditioning paths rather than ordinary image-to-image latent initialization. TI2V-5B VAE-encodes one input frame, masks first-frame timesteps, and reinserts the condition before decode. A14B I2V uses the separate `Wan-AI/Wan2.2-I2V-A14B-Diffusers` snapshot and concatenated image-condition latents. Multi-image/video interpolation is not enabled.

Example image-to-video command:

```sh
mlxgen generate \
  --model Wan-AI/Wan2.2-TI2V-5B-Diffusers \
  --task image-to-video \
  --image input.png \
  --prompt "A slow cinematic camera move from the input frame" \
  --width 1280 \
  --height 704 \
  --frames 121 \
  --steps 50 \
  --guidance 5 \
  --fps 24 \
  --output video.mp4
```

Example A14B image-to-video command:

```sh
mlxgen generate \
  --model Wan-AI/Wan2.2-I2V-A14B-Diffusers \
  --task image-to-video \
  --image input.png \
  --prompt "A cinematic flyby around the subject in the input image" \
  --width 1280 \
  --height 720 \
  --frames 81 \
  --steps 40 \
  --guidance 3.5 \
  --fps 16 \
  --output video.mp4
```

Wan2.2 T2V-A14B uses two transformer folders with Diffusers-compatible high-noise/low-noise boundary routing. `--guidance-2` is an optional low-noise-stage override. With no guidance flags, MLX-Gen uses the model's two-stage defaults (`4` high-noise and `3` low-noise for T2V-A14B). If `--guidance` is set and `--guidance-2` is omitted, the low-noise stage follows `--guidance`:

```sh
mlxgen generate \
  --model Wan-AI/Wan2.2-T2V-A14B-Diffusers \
  --task text-to-video \
  --prompt "A cinematic shot of mist rolling across a teal mountain lake" \
  --width 1280 \
  --height 720 \
  --frames 81 \
  --steps 40 \
  --guidance 4 \
  --guidance-2 3 \
  --fps 16 \
  --output video.mp4
```

The separate A14B I2V route targets `Wan-AI/Wan2.2-I2V-A14B-Diffusers` and uses concatenated image-condition latents. It requires the complete local source snapshot before generation.

Wan video progress is denoising-step based. The CLI keeps the requested frame count as context,
but `complete` is reserved for a video that has been saved and passed MP4 health validation. If Wan
generation or save validation fails, MLX-Gen writes a compact `<output>.failure.json` manifest next
to the intended output path.

Wan image-to-video responds best when the input image and prompt describe the same plausible motion. Use a source frame with the whole subject visible, margin around moving limbs or objects, and minimal occlusion. Match the source image's aspect ratio and composition to the requested video canvas; TI2V-5B normalizes dimensions to multiples of 32, so compose the source for the adjusted dimensions when a requested size is rounded. Keep identity-critical regions inside the rendered frame: once a face, hand, foot, logo, product edge, or other important region leaves the visible image, the model no longer has rendered pixels to anchor its identity or geometry, and re-entry may reconstruct it from latent and temporal context with drift. Also constrain rotation when front-facing identity matters: ask for front-facing or three-quarter-front motion, keep pivots below about 60-90 degrees, and block rear/back/profile-only views. Write the positive prompt as a concrete motion plan: name the subject, camera style, body parts or object parts that should move, and continuity constraints that should remain stable. For human motion, prompts should explicitly mention torso pivots, head turns, arm sweeps, leg crossing or weight shifts, full head and body visibility, attached arms, and natural hands and feet. For object motion, describe the physical action, such as a spacecraft lifting away from snow with engines firing, stable hull geometry, and no scene cut.

Use a negative prompt to block common image-to-video failure modes: `static still image`, `only arms moving`, `only camera movement`, cropped body parts, back to camera, rear view, turned away, profile-only view, over-rotated body, detached or disconnected arms and hands, broken wrists, malformed hands, oversized or melted feet, duplicate bodies, black or green frames, flicker, subject exits frame, and sudden scene cuts. Prompting reduces but does not eliminate model brittleness, especially around hands, wrists, feet, ankles, self-occluding poses, out-of-frame re-entry, and return from back-facing views. Prefer shorter clips, restrained motion, wider framing, camera-follow prompts, generous margins around raised hands and feet, rotation limits when face identity matters, or separate keyframed clips when the action would otherwise move identity-critical regions outside the rendered frame. For production checks, inspect decoded frames or contact sheets rather than relying only on MP4 existence.

Wan does not have a separate duration flag. Duration is `frames / fps`; at 24 fps, 121 frames is about 5.04 seconds, 73 frames is about 3.04 seconds, and 49 frames is about 2.04 seconds. Frame counts are normalized to `4n + 1`. Width and height are normalized to the selected Wan model's VAE/patch multiple: TI2V-5B uses multiples of 32, while A14B uses multiples of 16. Spatial-scale T2V and I2V sanity panels at 1280x704, 17 frames, and 20 steps live in `docs/assets/generation/`; they are coherent non-green outputs, not full quality benchmarks.

Generation commands replace the requested `--output` path by default. Use `--replace false` or `--no-replace` to preserve an existing file and save to the next suffixed path.

---

## Quantization

MLX-Gen prepares quantized model folders with the mflux/MLX layout used for local inference. These folders are designed for MLX-Gen and compatible mflux code, not Diffusers or Transformers `from_pretrained()` checkpoints.

The primary q8/q4 prepared-folder compatibility surface covers Qwen Image, Qwen Image Edit, ERNIE Image Turbo, FLUX.2 Klein, Z-Image/Z-Image Turbo, FIBO when source access is available, and Wan2.2 with a mixed q8/BF16 policy. Qwen and ERNIE q4 folders use mixed q4/q8 policies because fully q4 checkpoints showed unacceptable generation-quality loss. FLUX.2 and Z-Image use standard MLX quantization policies. Bonsai Image is not a prepared q4/q8 folder; the supported ternary checkpoint is a pre-packed 2-bit FLUX.2-derived transformer with a 4-bit Qwen3 text encoder and BF16 Flux2 VAE. q8 remains the closest quantized prepared-folder option to BF16 when memory allows. Bonsai 2-bit and mixed q4/q8 are similar in intent: both keep sensitive paths at a higher precision and ship the smallest layout that stayed in the same visual family as the higher-precision baseline.

Wan mixed q8/BF16 quantizes bulky transformer block linears and keeps the VAE, UMT5 text encoder,
Wan `condition_embedder.*`, Wan `proj_out`, scheduler metadata, tokenizer files, norms,
convolutions, and non-quantizable parameters at BF16. Published-card validation reports T2V-A14B
BF16 at 64.3 GiB disk, 33.0 GiB full-process physical peak, and 152.7 s for a 384x224 33-frame
12-step run; T2V-A14B mixed q8/BF16 at 39.7 GiB disk, 20.7 GiB physical peak, and 154.8 s; I2V-A14B
BF16 at 64.1 GiB disk, 33.7 GiB physical peak, and 228.2 s for a 384x384 33-frame 12-step run; and
I2V-A14B mixed q8/BF16 at 39.7 GiB disk, 21.5 GiB physical peak, and 242.2 s. Mixed q8/BF16 reduces
storage and measured full-process memory in these validation runs; it is not currently claimed as a
speed improvement.

Qwen Image and Qwen Image Edit use a mixed q4/q8 policy when prepared with `--quantize 4`:

- q4 for most Qwen transformer attention, feed-forward, and projection linears.
- q8 for Qwen `*.img_mod_linear` transformer modulation layers.
- q4 for group64-compatible Qwen text-encoder language linears.
- q8 for group64-compatible Qwen text-encoder visual linears.
- BF16 for the VAE, norms, embeddings, and linears that are not MLX group64-compatible.

This Qwen policy applies to q4 prepared folders only, including Qwen Image and Qwen Image Edit variants such as 2509 and 2511 edit checkpoints.

The q8 path was not changed by the mixed-q4 work. Qwen q8 uses the standard MLX-Gen/mflux quantization flow: quantizable modules are saved at 8-bit where supported, while VAE weights and non-quantizable layers remain BF16. Other model families use their existing model-specific quantization predicates.

ERNIE Image Turbo supports BF16, q8, and q4 text-to-image generation plus experimental single-image image-to-image. ERNIE image-to-image initializes text-to-image denoising from encoded image latents, so it is useful for fast stylization and guided variation but not as exact as Qwen Image Edit for layout-preserving edits. ERNIE q4 uses a model-specific mixed q4/q8 policy: q4 for transformer Q/K attention and transformer feed-forward modules; q8 for Mistral3 text/Prompt Enhancer linears plus ERNIE transformer V/O attention, conditioning/output linears, and VAE attention; BF16 for norms, convolutions, and other non-quantizable parameters. Default ERNIE downloads fetch the BF16 generation components MLX-Gen currently uses and do not fetch the Prompt Enhancer model unless `mlxgen download --all-files` is requested. Prepared ERNIE q8/q4 folders contain the generation stack for text-to-image and experimental single-image image-to-image, and do not bundle Prompt Enhancer files.

Bonsai local validation compares `prism-ml/bonsai-image-ternary-4B-mlx-2bit` against
FLUX.2 Klein 4B q8 on the same prompt, seed 42, guidance 1, and 4 steps. The Bonsai ternary
snapshot is about 3.6 GiB and averaged 2.92 s at 512px with 3.57 GiB peak RSS across three
cold-process repeats. The Klein 4B q8 baseline used the 22 GiB source cache with q8 applied at
runtime and averaged 3.55 s at 512px with 9.23 GiB peak RSS. At 1024px, Bonsai took 5.69 s with
3.60 GiB peak RSS; Klein 4B q8 took 6.81 s with 9.39 GiB peak RSS. Bonsai binary 1-bit has a
smaller cached snapshot, but does not run on stock MLX through 0.31.2 because 1-bit packed affine
matmul support is missing.

---

## Hugging Face Publishing

Prepared model folders include a generated Hugging Face `README.md` card. The card records the source model, compatibility notes, quantization policy, exact `mlx-gen` version used to generate it, and source license/access policy where MLX-Gen can infer it. Generated cards include `python -m pip install -U mlx-gen` in the usage block for copy/pasteable public installation; repository development and release workflows still use uv. Upload the folder with a command such as:

```sh
huggingface-cli upload AbstractFramework/qwen-image-edit-2511-4bit ./models/qwen-image-edit-2511-4bit .
```

Hugging Face collection membership is managed separately from model-card metadata. After upload, use the Hugging Face UI or `huggingface_hub.HfApi.add_collection_item` with the collection slug from the collection URL.

Generated cards mark Qwen, Z-Image, ERNIE Image Turbo, and FLUX.2 Klein 4B derivatives as Apache 2.0 when MLX-Gen can identify the source model family. FLUX.2 Klein 9B and FLUX.2 Klein base-9B derivatives are marked with the FLUX non-commercial license metadata and gated-access prompts. Configure Hugging Face repository gating separately when publishing those derivatives; model-card metadata alone does not enforce access control. Bonsai pre-packed repositories are not generated by `mlxgen prepare`.

---

## Python Integration

Python callers should download or prepare models before constructing runtime model objects.

```python
from mflux.models.common.download_policy import DownloadRequiredError
from mlxgen.models.z_image import ZImageTurbo

try:
    model = ZImageTurbo(quantize=8)
except DownloadRequiredError as exc:
    print(exc.download_command)
    raise
```

Applications should surface the exception message or command fields and stop the workflow.

MLX-Gen is intended to be the Apple Silicon / MLX dependency for AbstractVision while AbstractVision remains cross-platform.

Current responsibilities:

- AbstractVision owns provider-neutral request objects, artifact storage, capability checks, and AbstractCore plugin integration.
- MLX-Gen owns MLX model loading, model-family behavior, local quantized formats, and runtime compatibility fixes.
- MLX-Gen fails early when required local artifacts are missing so AbstractVision can surface an actionable remediation message.

MLX-Gen exposes one lightweight `ProgressEvent` type for application monitoring. Image model instances publish events through `model.callbacks.subscribe_progress(callback, task=...)`. Wan video generation supports the same subscription path and also accepts a direct `progress_callback` argument on `generate_video()` for one-shot handlers. `ProgressEvent.progress` is denoise-step progress (`step / total_steps`). Video events also include `frame`, `total_frames`, and `frame_progress` for output-frame context. For Wan2.2 I2V-A14B, use `ModelConfig.wan2_2_i2v_a14b()` or the `Wan-AI/Wan2.2-I2V-A14B-Diffusers` model name and pass `image_path` to `generate_video()`.

---

## Troubleshooting

If MLX-Gen says it will not download files during generation, run the command shown in the error and retry.

If a local path cannot be classified, add `--family`.

If `generate --path` fails, use `mlxgen prepare --model ... --path ... --quantize ...` to create a model folder or `--output` to choose the generated image or video path.

For Wan image-to-video prompts, describe concrete motion rather than only a visual style. Name the moving body parts or object parts, keep continuity constraints in the positive prompt, and put common failure modes such as `static still image`, `only camera movement`, `detached arm`, `malformed hands`, `oversized foot`, `black frames`, and `sudden scene cut` in the negative prompt.

If the requested `--output` image already exists, MLX-Gen replaces it by default. Use `--replace false` or `--no-replace` to write to the next suffixed filename instead.

If a LoRA is missing, download it or pass a local `.safetensors` path. Missing user-requested LoRAs are required and are not silently ignored.

If `HF_HUB_ENABLE_HF_TRANSFER=1` is explicitly enabled and fails because `hf_transfer` is missing, install MLX-Gen with:

```sh
uv tool install --upgrade mlx-gen --with hf_transfer
```

---

## Release

MLX-Gen publishes Python distributions to PyPI and attaches the same distributions to a GitHub Release through `.github/workflows/release.yml`.

PyPI trusted publishing must be configured for PyPI project `mlx-gen`, repository `lpalbou/mlx-gen`, workflow filename `release.yml`, and GitHub environment `pypi`. The workflow uses OpenID Connect through `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish`; it does not require a long-lived PyPI API token.

The release workflow runs fast tests on macOS, validates package name, version, changelog entry, tag name, and duplicate PyPI versions, builds distributions with `uv build`, validates them with `twine check`, creates a GitHub Release with the wheel and source distribution when publishing, and publishes the same artifacts to PyPI through trusted publishing.

Run a non-publishing rehearsal with:

```sh
gh workflow run release.yml \
  -f version=<version> \
  -f publish=false
```

The preferred release path is a version tag:

```sh
git tag v<version>
git push origin main --tags
```

---

## Project Participation

Contributors should use `uv`, keep pull requests focused, document user-facing behavior in the core docs, and prefer `mlxgen` commands in new documentation. Wan full-model parity checks are opt-in because they require the cached source snapshot:

```sh
MFLUX_RUN_LOCAL_WAN_PARITY=1 MFLUX_PRESERVE_TEST_OUTPUT=1 uv run pytest tests/wan/test_wan_local_parity.py -q
```

Those checks validate component parity for the Wan transformer, VAE encoder/decoder, prompt embeddings, scheduler replay, and a tiny latent-only CFG denoise loop. They do not replace visual review or decoded video quality checks.

Security issues should be reported privately through the repository security advisory flow when available. Do not include secrets, private model tokens, or proprietary model artifacts in public reports.

Participants are expected to be respectful, constructive, and focused on the work.
